section.title: The Whole World Accountable
section.sectionRationale: Paul answers the objections his argument has raised. What advantage has the Jew? Much, for they were entrusted with the very words of God. Does human unfaithfulness cancel God's faithfulness? Never; let God be true and every man a liar. Then Paul gathers a chain of Old Testament texts to prove the universal charge: none is righteous, no not one. The law speaks so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God, for by works of the law no one will be justified; through the law comes only the knowledge of sin.
section.thesis: God remains faithful and just even though every human being is a liar, and Scripture itself testifies that Jew and Greek alike are wholly under sin; therefore the law cannot justify anyone but instead silences every excuse and exposes the guilt of the whole world before God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the real advantage of the Jew and the unshakable faithfulness of God despite human unbelief; the catena of Scripture proving that no one is righteous; and the verdict that every mouth is stopped and no one is justified by works of the law, which only brings knowledge of sin.

units.label: God remains faithful and true
units.note: the advantage of the Jew, though every man a liar

verses.text: What, then, is the advantage of being a Jew? Or what is the value of circumcision?
Much in every way. First of all, they have been entrusted with the very {{logion|words}} of God.
What if some did not have faith? Will their lack of faith nullify God's {{pistis|faithfulness}}?
Certainly not! Let God be true and every man a liar. As it is written: “So that You may be proved right when You speak and victorious when You judge.”
But if our unrighteousness highlights the righteousness of God, what shall we say? That God is unjust to inflict His wrath on us? I am speaking in human terms.
Certainly not! In that case, how could God judge the world?
However, if my falsehood accentuates God's truthfulness, to the increase of His glory, why am I still condemned as a sinner?
Why not say, as some slanderously claim that we say, “Let us do evil that good may result”? Their condemnation is deserved!

units.label: None is righteous, no not one
units.note: the verdict of Scripture on the whole race

verses.text: What then? Are we any better? Not at all. For we have already made the charge that Jews and Greeks alike are all under {{hamartia|sin}}.
As it is written: “There is no one {{dikaios|righteous}}, not even one.
There is no one who understands, no one who seeks God.
All have turned away, they have together become worthless; there is no one who does good, not even one.”
“Their throats are open graves; their tongues practice deceit.” “The venom of vipers is on their lips.”
“Their mouths are full of cursing and bitterness.”
“Their feet are swift to shed blood;
ruin and misery lie in their wake,
and the way of peace they have not known.”
“There is no fear of God before their eyes.”

units.label: Every mouth stopped, the whole world accountable
units.note: no one justified by works of the law

verses.text: Now we know that whatever the law says, it says to those who are under the law, so that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God.
Therefore no one will be {{dikaioo|justified}} in His sight by works of the law. For the law merely brings awareness of sin.

logion.gloss: An oracle or divine utterance, the very words of God. Paul names Israel's chief advantage: they were entrusted with God's own revelation, the Scriptures. This is a stewardship, not a possession to boast in; being given the oracles of God magnifies both the privilege and the responsibility, and makes disbelief the more serious.

pistis.gloss: Faith or faithfulness. Here it names God's faithfulness, His covenant reliability, which human unbelief cannot cancel. The same word runs through the letter for the faith that receives God's righteousness; God's faithfulness and our faith belong to the one story of a God who keeps His word though every man prove false.

hamartia.gloss: Sin, a missing of the mark, understood by Paul not merely as scattered wrong acts but as a power that holds people captive. To be under sin is to be under its dominion and its guilt. Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, which is why the whole world stands accountable and needs a rescue from outside itself.

dikaios.gloss: Righteous, just, in right standing before God. The catena opens with the verdict there is no one righteous, not even one. No human being measures up to God's standard; the universal negative prepares for the good news that righteousness must come as a gift, not an achievement, through faith in Christ.

dikaioo.gloss: To justify, to declare righteous, a courtroom verdict of acquittal and right standing. Paul states the negative that governs the whole argument: no one will be justified by works of the law. The verb sets up the great positive of 3:21 following, that God justifies freely by grace through faith in Christ.

basic.context: Having shown that both the openly wicked and the religiously privileged stand condemned, Paul pauses to field objections. If the Jew is just as accountable, what advantage remains? And if human sin only shows off God's righteousness, is God unfair to judge? Paul answers, then delivers the summary of his whole indictment: a string of Scriptures proving that the entire human race is under sin.
basic.hermeneutics: The first half is diatribe, a rapid exchange of questions and answers with an imagined objector. The second half is a catena, a chain of Old Testament quotations woven together from the Psalms and Isaiah. Read the quotations as Paul's scriptural jury: he is not inventing a harsh verdict but showing that Israel's own Scriptures already pronounce the whole world guilty.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel's advantage is that they were entrusted with the logion, the oracles of God. Human unbelief cannot cancel God's pistis, His faithfulness. The charge is that all are under hamartia, sin as a ruling power. Scripture declares there is no one dikaios, righteous. And the conclusion follows: no one will be dikaioo, justified, by works of the law, which only brings knowledge of sin.
basic.typology: The words of God entrusted to Israel are the Scriptures that testify beforehand to Christ. The universal indictment recalls the flood generation and the tower of Babel, humanity together turned away, now shown to be the condition of every heart. The stopped mouth before God anticipates every knee bowing before the Judge.
basic.christ: Christ is the hidden answer to this passage. The whole point of proving universal guilt is to show that no one can be saved by the law, so that the righteousness of God in Christ, revealed in the very next verses, is the only hope. God's faithfulness, which no human unbelief can nullify, is kept supremely in sending His Son. The stopped mouth of the guilty is opened again only in Christ to confess and give thanks.
basic.god: God is faithful, true, and just. His reliability does not depend on human faith; though every person proves a liar, God stands proved right. He is the righteous Judge of all the world, and His judgment is entirely fair. He cannot be charged with injustice for punishing sin, and He will not be manipulated by the twisted logic that we should sin so grace may abound.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 51:4 stands behind verse 4, that God is proved right when He judges. Psalms 14 and 53, 5, 140, 10, 36, and Isaiah 59 supply the catena of verses 10 to 18. Galatians 2:16 restates that no one is justified by works of the law. Romans 3:23 sums it up: all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's storyline of human rebellion, running from Eden through the flood and Babel and Israel's own failures, is here gathered into one verdict: none is righteous, no not one. Every mouth is stopped and the whole world is guilty before God. This is the darkness against which the gospel shines, for the very next word is but now, the announcement of a righteousness from God given freely in Christ.

context.literary: Romans 3:1-20 concludes the long argument begun at 1:18 that all are under sin. It falls into a diatribe of questions and answers, verses 1 to 8, a catena of Scripture, verses 9 to 18, and a summary verdict, verses 19 to 20. The refrain not even one and the stopped mouth of verse 19 mark the climax of the indictment.
context.historical: Paul writes around AD 57 to a church of Jews and Gentiles in Rome. His argument that Jew and Gentile are equally guilty would raise sharp objections from those proud of covenant privilege. By quoting Israel's own Scriptures, Paul answers within the shared authority both groups acknowledged, letting the law testify against all.
context.canonical: This passage is the New Testament's fullest catena on human sinfulness, drawing the Psalms and Isaiah into a single indictment. It anchors the doctrine of universal depravity and functions canonically as the dark backdrop for the doctrine of justification by faith that immediately follows, and which the Reformers recovered as the heart of the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Paul intends to close every loophole, answering the objections that his gospel dishonors God or empties Jewish privilege, and then to prove from Scripture itself that the whole human race is under sin, so that the law is shown to condemn rather than to justify and the reader is left with no hope but grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the actual condition of humanity as declared by Scripture and the actual character of God as faithful and just. It prescribes the abiding truths that God is never unfaithful though we are, that sin is universal, and that no one can be justified by law keeping, so all must look to grace.

debates.question: Does universal sinfulness in verses 10 to 18 mean no one ever does any relative good?

views.label: Total inability, not utter depravity of every act
views.case: The historic Reformed reading holds that the texts describe total inability, that sin corrupts every part of a person and that no one is righteous before God or seeks Him, without claiming that unbelievers never do civic or relative good. No human work can secure acceptance with God.

views.label: Rhetorical universality
views.case: Some read the catena as strong prophetic hyperbole describing humanity as a whole and Israel's rebellion in particular, stressing the corporate verdict more than a claim about every individual act.

debates.bearing: Both readings uphold Paul's conclusion in verse 20: by works of the law no one will be justified. The point is not to measure degrees of goodness but to establish that no one can stand before God on the basis of works.

debates.question: What does it mean that through the law comes knowledge of sin in verse 20?

views.label: The law diagnoses but cannot cure
views.case: The traditional view is that the law reveals and defines sin, exposing guilt and increasing accountability, but has no power to remove sin or to justify. It is a mirror that shows the stain, not the water that washes it away.

views.label: The law also stirs up sin
views.case: Drawing on Romans 7, some add that the law not only reveals sin but provokes it, so that the commandment awakens rebellion, deepening the need for deliverance in Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way the law cannot save. Its function here is to bring awareness of sin and to silence every mouth, driving the guilty to the righteousness of God that comes apart from the law.

study.originalLanguages: Paul begins with Israel's advantage: they were entrusted with the logion, the very oracles of God. He defends God's pistis, His covenant faithfulness, which human unbelief cannot cancel; God stands proved right though every man is a liar. The charge is that all are under hamartia, sin as a dominating power, and Scripture confirms it: there is no one dikaios, righteous, not even one. The verdict follows inexorably: no one will be dikaioo, justified, before God by works of the law, for the law can only bring awareness of sin.

typology.fulfillment: The Scriptures entrusted to Israel testify beforehand to the gospel and find their yes in Christ, the living Word to whom they point.
The corporate rebellion of the flood and Babel generations is shown to be the condition of every heart, answered by the new humanity gathered in Christ.

typology.type: Every mouth stopped before the Judge
typology.fulfillment: The silencing of all boasting before God anticipates the day when every knee bows and every tongue confesses Jesus Christ as Lord.

study.christ: Christ stands just beyond the horizon of this passage as its whole aim. Paul proves the entire world guilty precisely so that the righteousness of God in Christ, unveiled in the very next verse, will be seen as the only refuge. God's faithfulness, which no human unfaithfulness can nullify, reaches its climax in the sending of His Son to keep every covenant promise. The mouth that is stopped in guilt is opened again only in Christ, no longer to make excuses but to confess sin and praise the God who justifies the ungodly. Every dark line here makes the grace of the gospel shine brighter.
study.god: This passage guards the character of God against every objection. He is faithful when His people are faithless, true when every man is a liar, and just in all His judgments; He is proved right precisely when He judges. He cannot be accused of injustice for punishing sin, nor be flattered by the argument that our sin serves His glory and so should be excused or multiplied. He is the righteous Judge of all the earth, whose verdict on the whole world is true, and whose faithfulness will keep every promise He has made.

crossReferences.note: That You may be proved right when You speak, David's confession, cited to show God is vindicated even in judgment.
There is no one who does good, the primary source of the catena on universal sinfulness.
Their feet are swift to shed blood and the way of peace they have not known, supplying verses 15 to 17.
By works of the law no one will be justified, Paul's parallel statement of the verdict of verse 20.

study.oneStory: From Eden to the flood to Babel to Israel's own repeated failures, the Bible tells of a race that has together turned away from God. Paul gathers that whole history into a single verdict drawn from Israel's Scriptures: none is righteous, no not one. Every mouth is stopped and the whole world is accountable to God. Yet this is the hinge of the story, for the darkness of universal guilt is exactly what makes the next words, a righteousness of God apart from the law, the best news the world has ever heard.

quiz.q: What does Paul name as the chief advantage of being a Jew?

options.text: They were entrusted with the very words, the oracles, of God.
options.note: Verse 2 says first of all they were entrusted with the very words of God.

options.text: They received and were made stewards of God's revelation.
options.note: Correct in substance; the text specifies this as the oracles or words of God.

options.text: They were guaranteed salvation by their descent from Abraham.
options.note: Descent gives no guarantee; Paul has just shown covenant privilege cannot save the disobedient.

options.text: They had no real advantage over the Gentiles at all.
options.note: Paul answers much in every way; the advantage is real, beginning with the oracles of God.

quiz.q: Does human unfaithfulness cancel God's faithfulness?

options.text: Certainly not; let God be true and every man a liar.
options.note: Verses 3 and 4 exactly: human unbelief cannot nullify God's faithfulness.

options.text: No; God stays reliable to His word even when people fail.
options.note: Right in meaning; Paul puts it as let God be true and every man a liar.

options.text: God's faithfulness partly depends on how His people respond.
options.note: Paul denies this; God's faithfulness does not rest on human faithfulness.

options.text: Yes; if people are unfaithful, God's promises fall to the ground.
options.note: Certainly not, says Paul; God remains true though every man is a liar.

quiz.q: What is the summary verdict of the Scripture chain in verses 10 to 18?

options.text: There is no one righteous, not even one; all have turned away.
options.note: The catena opens and hinges on this universal verdict.

options.text: Every person falls short and none seeks after God on his own.
options.note: Faithful to the sense; the text's sharp refrain is no one righteous, not even one.

options.text: Only the Gentiles are under sin, while Israel is exempt.
options.note: Paul insists Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, not the Gentiles alone.

options.text: Most people are basically good with only occasional faults.
options.note: The catena declares the opposite: none is righteous, no not one.

quiz.q: Why does the law speak its verdict, according to verse 19?

options.text: So that every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God.
options.note: Verse 19 states this purpose word for word.

options.text: So that all human boasting is stopped and everyone stands guilty before God.
options.note: Correct in meaning; the text speaks of every mouth silenced and the world accountable.

options.text: So that those under the law can earn acquittal by keeping it.
options.note: The reverse; verse 20 says no one is justified by works of the law.

options.text: So that the Gentiles alone are shown to be sinners.
options.note: The law silences every mouth and holds the whole world, not only Gentiles, accountable.

quiz.q: What does the law accomplish regarding justification, according to verse 20?

options.text: By works of the law no one is justified; the law brings knowledge of sin.
options.note: Verse 20 says exactly this: no justification by works, only awareness of sin.

options.text: The law cannot make anyone righteous; it only reveals their sin.
options.note: Right in substance; the text specifies no one is justified by works of the law.

options.text: The law justifies those who try their sincere best to keep it.
options.note: Paul denies this; sincere effort at law keeping cannot justify anyone.

options.text: The law removes sin from those who study it carefully.
options.note: The law brings awareness of sin; it has no power to remove it.

questions: Israel's great privilege was being entrusted with the words of God. How seriously do you treat the privilege of possessing the Scriptures, and does that privilege lead you to trust or to presume?
Paul insists that God stays faithful even when people are faithless. Where do you need to rest in God's reliability rather than in your own consistency?
Scripture declares there is no one righteous, not even one. How does an honest reckoning with your own sin, rather than comparing yourself to others, change how you see your need for grace?
Paul says every mouth is stopped before God. What excuses or self-justifications do you still bring before God that this passage silences?
The law can diagnose sin but cannot cure it. How does recognizing what the law cannot do sharpen your longing for the righteousness that comes through Christ?

translationNotes.note: The very words of God renders ta logia, which some translations give as the oracles of God. It refers to the whole of God's revelation entrusted to Israel, a stewardship rather than a boast.
So that You may be proved right when You speak quotes Psalm 51:4. The point is that God is vindicated, shown to be in the right, even and especially in His judgments.

Works of the law refers to deeds done in obedience to the Mosaic law. Paul's point, recovered by the Reformers, is that no such works can justify; the law's role is to bring awareness of sin, not acceptance with God.

Poetic quotation lines (stored as poem objects) were concatenated with spaces to form each verse; footnote markers and line breaks were rendered as spaces per format. No em or en dashes are present in this passage's BSB text. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
